_meta.description: Comprehensive English chart type dictionary with synonyms, data shape requirements, and best-use guidance.

avoidWhen: Multiple overlapping series that occlude each other
Values do not sum to a meaningful total
Too many overlapping points without transparency
Too many series that are hard to distinguish

bestFor: Comparing proportions over time or across groups
Showing composition changes over time as percentages

avoidWhen: Too many steps creating visual noise

bestFor: Highlighting overall direction over noisy data
Separating trend, seasonal, and residual components
Denser view than candlestick for long periods
avoidWhen: Small screens where tick marks are hard to read

bestFor: Combining box plot and density information
Showing every data point without overlap
Comparing sample distribution to a theoretical one
avoidWhen: Small samples where deviations are expected
Absolute values more important than gaps

bestFor: Dashboard KPI gauges without circular gauges
Many categories in a compact space
avoidWhen: Very few categories where bar chart is simpler

bestFor: Showing positive and negative deviations from a baseline
Showing relative sizes of nested categories

avoidWhen: Many small slices at outer rings

bestFor: More intuitive than pie for exact proportions
Showing joint distribution of two variables
Alternative to sunburst without angular distortion
Showing trajectory of two variables over time
Exploring pairwise relationships in multivariate data
Showing change between two time points

avoidWhen: Too many bubbles causing label overlap

bestFor: Showing cumulative effect of sequential values

avoidWhen: Simple two-stage flows where a bar chart suffices

bestFor: Explaining variance from start to end
Showing how categorical memberships change across stages

avoidWhen: Small matrices where a simple table suffices
Too many cells to render legibly

bestFor: Showing regional data variation on a map
avoidWhen: Large regions dominating visual despite low values

bestFor: Comparing group means with confidence intervals
avoidWhen: Asymmetric distributions shown as symmetric bars

bestFor: Comparing effect sizes with confidence intervals across studies
Assessing agreement between two measurement methods

avoidWhen: Absolute values more important than ranks

bestFor: Identifying clusters and outliers across dimensions
Comparing two time points or conditions
Displaying ordinal rankings with underlying values

avoidWhen: Both series have same unit and scale

bestFor: Comparing two series with different scales
Showing composition (stack) alongside a total or rate (line)
avoidWhen: Too many stacked series obscuring the line
Simple data not requiring dual encoding

bestFor: Emphasizing volume (area) with a benchmark line
avoidWhen: Line is completely hidden by area fill

bestFor: Comparing trends across many groups without overlap
Cleaner than many lines on one axis
Comparing distributions across groups in separate panels
avoidWhen: Simple comparison better served by grouped bar
Historical trend is more important than current state
